_description: OUTPUT CONTRACT (§8): a complete scored assessment that verifies ALL required output fields, rounding rules, and integrityFlags naming.

_contract.rounding: all scores rounded to one decimal place (not two, not zero)

signals.excerpt: Mission-linked roles attract more motivated volunteers because the values alignment is explicit from day one.
For smaller orgs I simplify the strategic planning to a one-page framework instead of a full business case.
I assessed organizational readiness before we expanded the tutoring program at UrbanReach last year.
Motivation-based matching works because functional motives predict persistence — a volunteer with career motive will disengage if the role offers no skill development.
For high-responsibility roles like crisis support I add a second screening conversation that lower-risk roles don't need.
At Greenway I ran staged onboarding: orientation, then role training, then 30-day check-in.
Training depth must match role risk — a crisis-line volunteer needs 12 hours minimum; an event greeter needs 30 minutes.
For tech-averse older volunteers I redesign training materials to use paper job aids.
I create growth options for experienced volunteers: skill expansion, leadership roles, mentoring assignments.
Setting expectations at the start prevents most performance issues — ambiguity is the root cause of volunteer underperformance more often than unwillingness.
I released a volunteer from a direct-service role respectfully last year after documented coaching didn't resolve the pattern.
For short-term volunteers I simplify the accountability conversation.
Generic recognition doesn't work because people have different motivational profiles — a career-motivated volunteer values skill endorsement more than a plaque.
For long-tenure volunteers I shift from gratitude recognition to growth opportunities.
I run exit conversations with every departing volunteer and use the data to adjust our program.
Access barriers systematically exclude volunteers from lower-income backgrounds — if we require daytime availability we're effectively selecting for the already-privileged.
For a population with transport barriers I ran a remote volunteer option — different ethics calculus for in-person vs. remote roles.
I applied ethical frameworks to a boundary situation last year when a volunteer developed a personal relationship with a client.

dimensions.name: Performance, Impact & Accountability
dimensions._score_calc: v1.1: Σ Proficient+: S3 clear=1.0, S2 clear=1.0, S4 weak=0.5 (at Proficient tier). Σ=2.5; Q=2.0, K=5.0 → position=(2.5−2.0)/3.0=0.167; score=5.6+0.167*1.9=5.92→5.9
dimensions._note_on_S4_weak: S4 at 'weak' (0.5) contributes if its anchor is at Proficient tier level. Same 'Σ at base tier and above' interpretation as v1.0 — only the Q offset changed.

_contract_verification_checklist: rubricVersion field present and equals '1.2' (string not number)
All 6 dimension objects present with ids D1–D6
Each dimension has: id, name, score, tier, baseTier, signals, redFlags, insufficientEvidence, evidenceDensity, appliedCaps
Each signal has: type, strength (numeric), excerpt, anchor, tier (matched anchor tier label)
strength in output is numeric (weak=0.5, clear=1.0, strong=1.5), not string
evidenceDensity has: sumStrength, K, Q, position
appliedCaps entries have: rule, capValue, reason; every applied §5.3/§5.4/§6/§7 cap is listed
All scores rounded to exactly 1 decimal place
overall has: score, tier, partial (bool), capped (bool), capReason (string or null)
integrityFlags is array (empty array when no flags, not null/absent); flags include triggering signal ids/excerpts
No extra fields that are not in the §8 contract
